context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole global actors/institutions thing first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through this! Before I explain anything, let's start with what you already know. When you hear 'global institutions,' what are one or two examples that come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation before engaging with the material themselves. Per the rules, I should not do the work for them or lecture them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and guide them toward their own understanding. I'll ask a simple opening question to get them thinking about what they already know, keeping my response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: uh maybe UN and World Bank? but idk what counts vs just “actors” lol
exchanges.tutor: Good examples! Now think about this: the UN and World Bank are organizations, but what about an individual country like the US, or a multinational corporation like Apple - would those count as 'actors' in development? What do you think makes something an 'actor' vs an 'institution'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified two key global institutions. I should affirm this briefly and then use a Socratic question to help them distinguish between 'institutions' and 'actors' themselves, rather than explaining the difference for them. This keeps the discovery student-led.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah? ngl it all kinda blends together rn, can u just give me a broad map first so I stop mixing everything up?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels blurry, but you're closer than you think! Try this: you just mentioned the UN, World Bank, the US, and Apple. Can you sort those into two or three groups based on what they have in common? Don't overthink it — just go with your gut.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a full explanation, which I should not do. However, I can acknowledge their confusion and redirect with a targeted Socratic question that helps them build their own conceptual map. Rather than giving them a taxonomy, I'll ask them to try sorting the examples they already mentioned, which will organically lead them to distinguish actors from institutions.

exchanges.student: ok maybe states, intl orgs, and companies. that didn’t fully help tho; I’m still stuck on the “scale/resources/agenda” parts fr
exchanges.tutor: That typology is actually a solid starting point — hold onto it, it's directly relevant to the assignment. Let's tackle scale first. Take the World Bank: does it operate at a city level, a national level, a regional level, or something bigger? What makes you say that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid basic typology (states, international orgs, companies) which is a good start and directly relevant to the prompt's question about a 'useful typology.' I should affirm this briefly as it's genuinely useful progress, then redirect them to the prompt's questions one at a time rather than tackling all three at once. I'll pick the most foundational question - scale - to start with, using a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: bigger than national i guess, but also it works thru countries? tbh can u just show me how all the prompt parts fit together in one frame?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation - operating globally but channeling through national governments is a key tension worth writing about. Now, when the World Bank works through a country, what do you think it's actually bringing to the table? Money, sure - but what else might it be offering or imposing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to synthesize everything for them, which I should refuse. However, they did make a genuinely insightful observation about scale - that the World Bank operates above the national level but works through countries. I should affirm this insight and use it as a bridge to the next prompt question about resources, keeping momentum going rather than letting them stall. I'll redirect with a targeted question instead of providing a full framework.

exchanges.student: maybe money, policy ideas, conditions, data? idk if that’s enough tho, and I’m still not seeing how to turn it into 700-800 words.
exchanges.tutor: Money, policy ideas, conditions, data - look at the prompt again. It asks about 'economic, legal, political and cultural resources.' Can you match what you just listed to those four categories? That alone is a section of your paper. Then there's still the question of how the 'development agenda' gets set - who do you think decides what development even means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now organically identified several key resource types (money, policy ideas, conditions, data) which map well onto the prompt's economic, legal, political, and cultural resources. I should briefly affirm this and connect it back to the prompt's language to help them see they're building their answer. I should also address their concern about length by nudging them toward the last part of the prompt - how the development agenda emerges - since they haven't engaged with that yet. I won't give them a framework or outline.

exchanges.student: maybe donors and big institutions decide? but I’m prob missing half of it, can u just give me a simple outline/example angle pls
exchanges.tutor: That instinct about donors and big institutions shaping the agenda is worth developing - think about whose priorities get centered and whose don't. You've actually already touched on every part of the prompt in our conversation. Before asking for more, try writing 2-3 sentences summarizing what you'd say for each prompt question. That's your outline - built by you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an outline, which I should not provide as it would do the writing work for them. However, they've made good progress and have now touched on all the major components of the prompt organically across our conversation. This is a good moment to ask them to consolidate what they've built so far into their own rough plan, rather than me giving them one. I should also acknowledge their insight about donors/institutions setting the agenda as a valid and important point worth developing.

exchanges.student: i can try, but rn I mostly want a sample thesis bc I’m still fuzzy tbh. trying diff angle since the outline thing didn’t click.
exchanges.tutor: Writing a thesis for you isn't something I can do - that's your job! But here's a nudge: a thesis for this prompt would need to take a position on how global actors shape development. Based on everything we've discussed, what's your actual opinion - do you think the current setup of global institutions is fair to developing countries, or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for a sample thesis, which I should not provide as it constitutes doing the writing work for them. They are also trying a different angle to get me to produce content, which I should gently but firmly redirect. Per the rules, I do not write, suggest language, or provide examples of student-facing content. I should hold the boundary clearly but constructively, and redirect them toward formulating their own thesis by asking a focused question.

exchanges.student: not really fair imo, but I need help phrasing it less basic lol. can u help me make it sound more academic?
exchanges.tutor: Polishing your language isn't my role - that has to come from you. But 'not fair' is a starting point. What specifically makes it unfair? Think about what we discussed: who sets the agenda, what conditions come with resources, whose priorities get centered. Can you be more specific about the mechanism of that unfairness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to polish or improve their language, which the rules explicitly prohibit. I should clearly decline and redirect them toward developing their own thinking rather than their phrasing. The student has a genuine position now though, which is progress. I should push them to add specificity to their idea - what makes it unfair, according to what they've discussed - so they can write a more substantive thesis themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe like, powerful actors define development and weaker countries have to adapt? that still sounds super plain tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong core argument - don't underestimate it. Now, can you connect it to your typology (states, orgs, companies) and the resources you identified? How does each actor type contribute to that dynamic? Try writing a few sentences - rough is fine - and you'll see it's less plain than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a substantive and accurate core argument - that powerful actors define development and weaker countries must adapt. This is genuinely good thinking and maps well to the prompt's question about how the development agenda emerges from dominant global actors. Per the rules, I cannot help them phrase it more academically. I should affirm the substance of their idea and push them to now connect it to their typology and resources discussion, so they can see how their thesis anchors the whole piece. We're close to the end of our planned 10 exchanges, so I should also nudge them toward writing.
